definition: A missing value is an absent or unrecorded entry in a feature; handling it means detecting gaps, classifying why they occur (MCAR, MAR, MNAR), and choosing deletion or imputation while fitting all statistics on training data only.
layman: Missing values are blank spots in your spreadsheet—empty cells, "N/A", or sensor dropouts. You first notice where data is missing, think about whether the blanks are random or meaningful, then either drop those rows, fill gaps with a sensible default (like median age), or use a model that can work around holes.
content:
## What counts as missing?

A **missing value** is any observation that was not recorded, was lost in transfer, or was intentionally withheld. In tabular data it often appears as `NaN`, `null`, empty strings, or **sentinel codes** (e.g. `999`, `-1`) that stand in for "unknown."

Missing-value handling has three stages:

1. **Audit** — measure how much is missing per column and per row.
2. **Diagnose** — reason about **why** values are missing (this guides safe treatment).
3. **Treat** — delete rows/columns, impute, or use learners that tolerate gaps.

### Why missingness matters

Ignoring gaps does not make them disappear. Many algorithms reject `NaN` outright; others silently misread sentinels as real numbers. Wrong treatment can **distort distributions**, **shrink sample size**, or cause **data leakage** when imputation uses holdout statistics.

### MCAR, MAR, and MNAR

Statisticians classify missingness mechanisms:

| Mechanism | Meaning | Practical intuition |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **MCAR** (Missing Completely At Random) | Probability of missing does not depend on observed or unobserved values | A lab instrument randomly fails 2% of the time |
| **MAR** (Missing At Random) | Missingness depends only on **observed** data, not on the missing value itself | Men skip optional income more often than women, but among men with the same education, missing income is random |
| **MNAR** (Missing Not At Random) | Missingness depends on the **unobserved** value | High earners decline to report income; the missingness is tied to the hidden number |

You rarely **prove** which case holds, but the classification steers method choice. **Listwise deletion** is less biased under MCAR; under MAR, **model-based imputation** using observed covariates can be reasonable; under MNAR, naive mean imputation can be systematically wrong and you may need **sensitivity analysis**, **missingness indicators**, or **specialized models**.

### Treatment strategies (canonical for this topic)

| Strategy | What it does | When it helps |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Listwise deletion** | Drop any row with a missing value in used columns | MCAR with low missing rate and plenty of rows remain |
| **Column drop** | Remove features with very high missing % | Column is mostly empty and not worth imputing |
| **Mean / median / mode impute** | Replace gaps with a single summary statistic | Quick baseline; numeric → median often safer than mean for skew |
| **Constant / placeholder** | Fill with a fixed value (e.g. `0`, `"Unknown"`) | Categorical gaps; pair with explicit category |
| **KNN / model imputation** | Predict missing cells from other features | MAR-ish patterns where other columns explain the gap |
| **Forward / backward fill** | Carry last (or next) observed value along a sorted axis | Time series and ordered logs |
| **Missingness indicator** | Add a binary flag "was this imputed?" | MNAR suspicion; preserves signal that something was absent |
| **Native missing support** | Train models that split on NaN (e.g. gradient-boosted trees) | Sparse numeric features where imputation would blur structure |

### Pipeline discipline

Fit imputation statistics—means, medians, KNN neighbors, MICE models—on **training data only**, then apply the same learned values to validation, test, and production. Recomputing medians on the full dataset before splitting causes **data leakage**, the same failure mode as fitting scalers on all rows.

Typical order in a numeric pipeline: **impute → encode → scale → model**. Scaling before imputation forces the scaler to see `NaN`; imputing first yields finite values for standardization.

### Connection to modeling

**Linear models**, **SVMs**, and **neural nets** generally need complete numeric inputs. **XGBoost** and **LightGBM** learn default directions for NaN during tree splits, which can outperform careless mean fill when missingness is informative—but they do not remove the need for thoughtful preprocessing on mixed pipelines.

For this topic you own the canonical definitions of **MCAR / MAR / MNAR**, **imputation strategies**, and **train-only fitting** for imputers. Other topics link here rather than re-deriving those rules.
keyPoints: Missing values may appear as NaN, null, blanks, or sentinel codes—audit before treating.
MCAR: missingness unrelated to data; MAR: depends on observed fields; MNAR: depends on the hidden value.
Imputation options include deletion, mean/median/mode, KNN, forward-fill, indicators, and model-native NaN handling.
Fit imputation statistics on training data only to avoid leakage.
Impute before scaling in typical numeric pipelines; tree models can learn NaN split directions.
